cards.definition: how high or low a note sounds — determined by the frequency of vibration
the volume of music — from ppp (very quiet) to fff (very loud)
the speed of the music — measured in BPM (beats per minute)
the pattern of long and short sounds and silences in music
a sequence of pitches that form a recognisable tune — the 'singable' part
two or more notes sounding together — chords, consonance, or dissonance
how musical layers are combined — monophonic, homophonic, or polyphonic
the tone colour or quality of a sound — what makes a trumpet sound different from a violin
how a piece of music is organised — e.g. binary (AB), ternary (ABA), rondo (ABACADA)
the key or tonal centre of a piece — major (bright/happy) or minor (dark/sad)

questions.question: What is dynamics in music?
options: A) The speed of the music
B) The volume of music — from very quiet to very loud
C) The pitch of notes
D) The rhythm pattern
questions.explanation: Dynamics describe volume: ppp (pianissimo) = very quiet, pp = quite quiet, p = quiet, mf = moderately loud, f = loud, ff = very loud, fff = extremely loud.

questions.question: What is tempo?
options: A) The volume of music
B) The rhythm of a melody
C) The speed of music — measured in BPM
D) The pitch of notes
questions.explanation: Tempo is the speed of the music. Italian terms: Largo (very slow), Andante (walking pace), Moderato (moderate), Allegro (fast), Presto (very fast).

questions.question: What is texture in music?
options: A) The timbre of instruments
B) How musical layers are combined — monophonic, homophonic, polyphonic
C) The structure of a piece
D) The key of a piece
questions.explanation: Texture describes how musical lines combine: monophonic (melody only), homophonic (melody + accompaniment), polyphonic (multiple independent melodies).

questions.question: What is timbre?
options: A) The speed of the music
B) The volume of music
C) The tone colour or quality of a sound
D) The rhythm of a piece
questions.explanation: Timbre (tone colour) is what makes different instruments sound distinct even at the same pitch — e.g. a flute sounds different from an oboe.

questions.question: What is tonality?
options: A) The rhythm pattern of a piece
B) The structure of a piece
C) The key or tonal centre — major or minor
D) The volume of a piece
questions.explanation: Tonality refers to the key of a piece. Major keys have a bright, happy quality; minor keys have a darker, sadder quality.

questions.question: What is harmony in music?
options: A) A single melodic line
B) Two or more notes sounding together — chords, consonance, or dissonance
C) The speed of music
D) The texture of a piece
questions.explanation: Harmony is created when two or more notes sound simultaneously. Consonant harmony sounds stable and pleasant; dissonant harmony sounds tense and unresolved.

questions.question: What is structure in music?
options: A) The volume of music
B) The timbre of instruments
C) How a piece is organised — e.g. binary, ternary, rondo
D) The pitch of notes
questions.explanation: Common musical structures: binary (AB), ternary (ABA), rondo (ABACADA), sonata form (exposition, development, recapitulation), verse-chorus.

questions.question: What is a melody?
options: A) The rhythm pattern of a piece
B) A sequence of pitches forming a recognisable tune
C) The combination of musical layers
D) The volume of a piece
questions.explanation: A melody is a linear sequence of pitches — the main tune that can be sung or played. Characteristics: contour, range, phrase length, intervals.

questions.question: What is rhythm?
options: A) The pitch of a note
B) The pattern of long and short sounds and silences
C) The key of a piece
D) How musical layers combine
questions.explanation: Rhythm is the temporal organisation of music — patterns of long/short notes and silences that give music its pulse and drive.

questions.question: What is pitch?
options: A) The volume of a note
B) The speed of music
C) How high or low a note sounds
D) The tone quality of an instrument
questions.explanation: Pitch is determined by frequency — higher frequency = higher pitch. Notes are named A-G and can be sharp (#) or flat (♭).

questions.question: Dynamics refers to the speed of music.
questions.explanation: Dynamics refers to VOLUME (loud/quiet). Tempo refers to speed.

questions.question: Polyphonic texture involves multiple independent melodic lines.
questions.explanation: Polyphonic texture: multiple independent melodies sounding simultaneously — e.g. a Bach fugue.

questions.question: Timbre is what makes a trumpet sound different from a violin.
questions.explanation: Timbre (tone colour) is the distinctive quality of a sound — determined by overtones and the way the instrument produces sound.

questions.question: A major key typically sounds dark and sad.
questions.explanation: Major keys typically sound bright and happy. Minor keys have a darker, sadder quality.

questions.question: Monophonic texture has only a single melodic line with no accompaniment.
questions.explanation: Monophonic texture = melody alone, no harmony or accompaniment.

questions.question: Harmony is created when two or more notes sound together.
questions.explanation: Harmony includes chords, intervals, and the relationship between simultaneous pitches.

questions.question: Ternary structure follows the pattern ABA.
questions.explanation: Ternary form: A section (first theme) → B section (contrasting) → return of A.

questions.question: Tempo is measured in beats per minute (BPM).
questions.explanation: BPM measures how many beats occur in one minute. 60 BPM = 1 beat per second.

questions.question: Dissonant harmony sounds stable and resolved.
questions.explanation: Dissonant harmony sounds tense and unresolved. CONSONANT harmony sounds stable and pleasant.

questions.question: Rhythm involves patterns of long and short sounds and silences.
questions.explanation: Rhythm is the organisation of notes and rests in time — giving music its pulse and feel.

questions.question: ________ is the volume of music — from ppp (very quiet) to fff (very loud).
questions.explanation: Italian dynamic markings: p = piano (quiet), f = forte (loud), mf = mezzo forte (moderately loud).

questions.question: ________ is the speed of music — measured in beats per minute.
questions.explanation: Italian tempo markings: Andante (walking), Allegro (fast), Largo (very slow).

questions.question: ________ describes how musical layers combine — monophonic, homophonic, or polyphonic.
questions.explanation: Texture tells us how many and what kind of melodic lines are present.

questions.question: ________ is the tone colour or quality of a sound — what makes instruments sound distinctive.
questions.explanation: Timbre is determined by the overtones produced when an instrument creates a note.

questions.question: ________ is the key or tonal centre — major (bright) or minor (dark).
questions.explanation: Major and minor are the two main tonalities in Western music.

questions.question: ________ is two or more notes sounding together — chords, consonance, or dissonance.
questions.explanation: Consonant harmony is stable; dissonant harmony creates tension.

questions.question: ________ is the organisation of a piece — binary (AB), ternary (ABA), rondo.
questions.explanation: Structure gives music its shape and helps listeners follow its journey.

questions.question: ________ is a sequence of pitches forming a recognisable tune.
questions.explanation: The melody is the foreground tune — often the most memorable part of a piece.

questions.question: ________ is the pattern of long and short sounds and silences.
questions.explanation: Rhythm gives music its energy and pulse.

questions.question: ________ is how high or low a note sounds — determined by frequency.
questions.explanation: Higher frequency = higher pitch. Pitches are named A through G.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: volume — from ppp (very quiet) to fff (very loud)
speed of music — measured in BPM
tone colour — what makes instruments sound distinctive

cards.definition: c.1600–1750 — features: complex counterpoint, ornamentation, basso continuo. Composers: Bach, Handel
c.1750–1820 — features: clear melody, balanced phrases, sonata form. Composers: Mozart, Haydn
c.1820–1900 — features: emotional expression, larger orchestras, programme music. Composers: Beethoven, Brahms
1900–2000 — features: atonality, serialism, minimalism, jazz, blues. Varied and experimental
Classical structure: exposition (introduces themes), development (manipulates them), recapitulation (restates them)
two or more independent melodic lines played simultaneously — associated with Baroque music
Baroque bass line — typically harpsichord + cello providing harmonic foundation
Romantic music depicting a story, scene, or idea — e.g. Vivaldi's The Four Seasons
20th century style — very gradual change, repetition of small cells, phase shifting. Composers: Steve Reich, Philip Glass
a minor pentatonic scale with an added flattened 5th (the blue note) — characteristic of blues and jazz

questions.question: What characterises Baroque music?
options: A) Simple melodies and balanced phrases
B) Complex counterpoint, ornamentation, and basso continuo
C) Large orchestras and emotional expression
D) Atonality and serialism
questions.explanation: Baroque music (c.1600–1750): intricate counterpoint, ornamental decoration, basso continuo (harpsichord+cello bass), and clarity of texture. Composers: Bach, Handel.

questions.question: What is sonata form?
options: A) A solo instrument playing a sonata
B) A Baroque dance form
C) Classical structure: exposition, development, recapitulation
D) A minimalist compositional technique
questions.explanation: Sonata form: exposition (first and second subject themes introduced), development (themes manipulated), recapitulation (themes restated, both in home key).

questions.question: What is programme music?
options: A) Music following a strict classical form
B) Music depicting a story, scene, or idea outside the music
C) Music for a specific instrument
D) Music with a basso continuo
questions.explanation: Programme music (Romantic era) has extra-musical associations — Vivaldi's Four Seasons depicts seasons, Berlioz's Symphonie Fantastique tells a story.

questions.question: What is minimalism in music?
options: A) Very simple Classical music
B) Music using very few instruments
C) A 20th-century style using repetition, gradual change, and phase shifting
D) Baroque counterpoint reduced to one melody
questions.explanation: Minimalism: Steve Reich and Philip Glass use repeating melodic cells with very gradual changes over long periods — creating hypnotic, meditative effects.

questions.question: What is counterpoint?
options: A) A single melodic line
B) Two or more independent melodies played simultaneously
C) A Romantic orchestral texture
D) A 20th-century technique
questions.explanation: Counterpoint is the combination of independent melodic lines — central to Baroque music. A fugue is a strict form of counterpoint.

questions.question: What is basso continuo?
options: A) A solo bass instrument
B) A continuous melodic line in the treble
C) Baroque bass line — harpsichord + cello providing harmonic foundation
D) A type of bass drum pattern
questions.explanation: Basso continuo is foundational in Baroque music — the harpsichord fills in harmonies (realising the figured bass) while the cello reinforces the bass line.

questions.question: What characterises Romantic music?
options: A) Simplicity and short works
B) Restrained, formal structures
C) Emotional expression, larger orchestras, and programme music
D) Atonality and experimental techniques
questions.explanation: Romantic music (c.1820–1900): expanded orchestras, emotional expressiveness, programme music, longer forms, chromatic harmony. Composers: Beethoven, Brahms, Tchaikovsky.

questions.question: What is the blues scale?
options: A) A major scale used in blues
B) A minor pentatonic scale with an added flattened 5th (blue note)
C) A chromatic scale for jazz
D) A scale unique to classical music
questions.explanation: The blues scale adds a flattened 5th (the 'blue note') to the minor pentatonic scale — this note creates the characteristic tension of blues and jazz.

questions.question: What characterises Classical period music?
options: A) Complex counterpoint and ornamentation
B) Extreme emotional expression and large orchestras
C) Clear melody, balanced phrases, and sonata form
D) Atonality and experimental structures
questions.explanation: Classical music (c.1750–1820): clarity, balanced phrases, homophonic texture, sonata form. Composers: Mozart, Haydn, early Beethoven.

questions.question: What characterises 20th century music?
options: A) Strict adherence to sonata form
B) Basso continuo and counterpoint
C) Diversity — atonality, serialism, minimalism, jazz, blues
D) Emotional expression with large orchestras
questions.explanation: The 20th century saw enormous diversity: atonality (Schoenberg), serialism, neo-classicism, jazz, blues, minimalism, electronic music. No single style dominated.

questions.question: Baroque music is associated with simple melodies and small orchestras.
questions.explanation: Baroque music features complex COUNTERPOINT and basso continuo — it is intricate and ornate.

questions.question: Sonata form consists of exposition, development, and recapitulation.
questions.explanation: Sonata form: expose themes → develop and manipulate → restate in home key.

questions.question: Programme music tells a story or depicts an idea outside the music itself.
questions.explanation: Romantic programme music: Vivaldi's Four Seasons, Berlioz's Symphonie Fantastique.

questions.question: Minimalism uses rapid change and variety.
questions.explanation: Minimalism uses GRADUAL change and repetition — evolving slowly over long time spans.

questions.question: Counterpoint involves a single melodic line.
questions.explanation: Counterpoint combines TWO or MORE independent melodic lines simultaneously.

questions.question: Romantic music is characterised by restrained, formal structures.
questions.explanation: Romantic music features EMOTIONAL EXPRESSION, larger orchestras, and less formal constraints.

questions.question: The blues scale includes a flattened 5th note.
questions.explanation: The blue note (flattened 5th) is the characteristic note of the blues scale.

questions.question: Classical period music features complex counterpoint.
questions.explanation: Counterpoint is associated with the BAROQUE period. Classical music features clearer, homophonic textures.

questions.question: Basso continuo is a feature of Baroque music.
questions.explanation: Basso continuo (harpsichord + cello) provides the harmonic and bass foundation in Baroque music.

questions.question: Steve Reich and Philip Glass are associated with minimalism.
questions.explanation: Both composers use repetitive cells and gradual change — defining features of minimalism.

questions.question: ________ music (c.1600–1750) features counterpoint, ornamentation, and basso continuo.
questions.explanation: Baroque composers include Bach and Handel.

questions.question: ________ music (c.1750–1820) features clear melody, balanced phrases, and sonata form.
questions.explanation: Classical composers include Mozart and Haydn.

questions.question: ________ music (c.1820–1900) features emotional expression and programme music.
questions.explanation: Romantic composers include Beethoven, Brahms, and Tchaikovsky.

questions.question: Sonata form: ________, development, recapitulation.
questions.explanation: The exposition introduces the first and second subject themes.

questions.question: ________ music depicts a story or idea outside the music — e.g. Vivaldi's Four Seasons.
questions.explanation: Programme music is associated with the Romantic period.

questions.question: ________ is two or more independent melodic lines sounding simultaneously.
questions.explanation: Counterpoint is central to Baroque music — the fugue is a strict contrapuntal form.

questions.question: ________ continuo provides the harmonic foundation in Baroque music.
questions.explanation: Typically harpsichord filling in harmonies with cello reinforcing the bass.

questions.question: ________ uses repetition and very gradual change — Steve Reich and Philip Glass.
questions.explanation: Minimalism creates hypnotic, meditative effects through slowly evolving repeated cells.

questions.question: The ________ scale adds a flattened 5th (blue note) to the minor pentatonic.
questions.explanation: The blue note creates the characteristic tension of blues and jazz.

questions.question: The 20th century saw enormous diversity — including ________, serialism, and minimalism.
questions.explanation: Atonality abandons the traditional tonal centre — Schoenberg's twelve-tone technique.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: c.1750–1820 — clear melody, balanced phrases, sonata form
repetition and gradual change — Reich, Glass
